turns.content: Can you explain Newton's third law to me? I understand the words but I can't visualize what it actually means.

The textbook phrasing 'every action has an equal and opposite reaction' makes it sound like forces cancel out, which confuses people. Better way to think about it: forces always come in pairs, and those pairs always act on *different* objects. When you push a wall, the wall pushes back on you with equal force — but the force on the wall and the force on you are separate events happening to separate objects.
Okay but if the forces are equal, why does anything ever move? If I push a box and the box pushes back on me equally, shouldn't everything stay still?

This is exactly the right confusion to have — it means you are thinking carefully. The key: the two forces act on *different objects*, so you cannot add them together to get zero. You push the box forward (force on the box). The box pushes you backward (force on you). These are two separate force equations. Whether the box moves depends only on forces acting on the box — including friction, its mass, and your push. Whether you slide backward depends only on forces acting on you. They are analysed separately.
Oh! So when I analyse the box's motion, I only look at forces on the box, not the reaction force?

Exactly right. You have just understood free body diagrams intuitively. In a free body diagram, you pick one object, draw only the forces acting ON that object, and apply F=ma. You never include the reaction force in the same diagram because it acts on a different object. This is one of those concepts where once it clicks, it stays with you.
